input.background: If cat ownership and dog ownership were independent, then \operatorname{Pr}(A$ and $B)$ would equal \operatorname{Pr}(A) \times \operatorname{Pr}(B)=0.35 \times 0.30=0.105.
input.question: However, in our example, \operatorname{Pr}(A$ and $B)=0.15.

output.method: 事件发生概率及独立性
output.answer: Therefore, these events are not independent.

Pr(A).description: The probability of owning a cat.

Pr(B).description: The probability of owning a dog.

Pr(A and B)_expected.description: The expected probability of owning both a cat and a dog if the events are independent.

Pr(A and B)_observed.description: The observed probability of owning both a cat and a dog.

meta_info.caption: Independence (Pet ownership example).
meta_info.section: 5.5  More RULES AND PROPERTIES OF PROBABILITY
